ISTE26AI101.project_name: A device powered by artificial intelligence technology to save the lives of students from suffocation on school buses
ISTE26AI101.ai_tools_used: My artificial intelligence tools are erdino smart type SP32 description which has WiFi two thermal sensors A and B a base for installing tools and parts a small screen LCD memory card for storing software tools

team_story.value:
The Pulse Of Life team of artificial intelligence technologies

Al-Rawad private schools-Khamis Mushait-Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
Secondary section

Project idea

Our team noticed that there is a serious problem in the Arabian Gulf region, a problem that may be Global, which is that one of the students is forgotten inside the school bus after the trip is over. In some cases, the student may fall asleep inside the bus, and the driver or supervisor cannot notice him, which leads to the bus being closed and the student being left inside. With high temperatures and lack of oxygen inside the closed bus, a choking disaster may occur that may â€“ God forbid â€“ lead to the death of the student before he is rescued.

Based on our humanitarian responsibility, we have created an intelligent system based on artificial intelligence technologies with the aim of protecting the lives of students and preventing such accidents from happening.

The mechanism of action of the system

The innovation is based on an Arduino smart device with Wi-Fi technology, as well as two thermal sensors (A and B) installed at the bus door, and a display to indicate the number of students inside the bus.

The idea of the system is based on the intelligent counting of students by the order of their passage through the sensors:

When the student enters, he passes through sensor A and then B, and the device adds (1) to the counter shown on the screen.

When the student exits, he passes through sensor B and then A, and the device deducts (1) from the counter.

Thus, the number of students inside the bus is calculated accurately moment by moment.

Safety mechanism

The system displays on the screen the number of students remaining inside the bus, and the driver cannot close the bus before making sure that the counter is equal to (0). If any number appears greater than zero, this indicates the presence of a student inside the bus, which alerts the driver to the need to check before departure.

The goal of the project

The "Pulse Of Life" project aims to strengthen the safety of school transport, prevent choking accidents caused by students forgetting inside buses, and contribute to saving lives using modern artificial intelligence technologies.

ISTE26AI102.ai_tools_used: ChatGPT was a key tool throughout the development of our robot, assisting with both hardware and software. It helped us troubleshoot Python code in Thonny on the Raspberry Pi, especially for the camera module and emotion detection, and guided us in using libraries like OpenCV, NumPy, TensorFlow/Keras, Picamera2, and Flask to process images, run the model, and stream the video feed to our MIT App Inventor app. We also used Arduino code to control the robotâ€™s components, with ChatGPT helping debug and understand connections. The app communicated with the robot via the HC-05 Bluetooth module, allowing wireless control, while medo.dev was used to create an AI-assisted website to present and organize our project. ChatGPT further supported us in hardware assembly by clarifying component connections and troubleshooting issues, enabling us to efficiently integrate the robotâ€™s software, hardware, and control systems into a functional project.

scores.comments_panel:
AEGIS is an ambitious and technically sophisticated project that demonstrates genuine understanding of a real community health problem. The app's use of NASA satellite data (MODIS NDVI, GPM precipitation, and land surface temperature) to generate dengue fever risk assessments for specific neighborhoods in Quezon City, Philippines reflects meaningful research and a credible technical approach. The mobile UI shows clear, actionable output organized for a non-technical audience, and the Android platform choice is appropriate for the intended community.
However, several gaps made consistent scoring difficult across multiple criteria. The GitHub repository contains only the compiled .apk file with no documentation, no README, and no instructions for how others can access or use the app — limiting the panel's ability to independently verify functionality. The pitch video, while polished, focuses almost entirely on what the app produces rather than how the team built it. There is no visible evidence of user testing with intended beneficiaries, no expert consultation referenced, and no reflection on iteration or design decisions that changed over time. Additionally, while the app's use of objective satellite data inherently reduces certain bias risks, the team does not demonstrate awareness of ethical considerations such as data coverage limitations, access equity, or user privacy around location input.
The project shows real promise and genuine technical skill. Stronger documentation, a walkthrough of the user input process, and explicit reflection on the team's learning journey would meaningfully strengthen this submission.
scores.comments_student:
What you have built with AEGIS is genuinely impressive. Using real NASA satellite data (MODIS NDVI, GPM precipitation, and land surface temperature) to assess dengue fever risk at the neighborhood level in Quezon City shows that your team did serious research and made thoughtful technical choices. The fact that you identified three specific environmental indicators that are scientifically connected to disease outbreak risk demonstrates a level of rigor that goes well beyond what most student teams attempt.
The app's output screen as shown in the video is a standout strength. The way you organized the recommendations into Immediate Actions, Prevention Measures, Community Awareness, and Resource Allocation makes the AI's analysis immediately useful to someone on the ground  without requiring any technical background to understand. That is excellent design thinking.
As you continue developing AEGIS, the area that would most strengthen the project is telling the story of how you built it, not just what it does. Future versions of your pitch would benefit from walking viewers through the user input experience, sharing feedback you received from community members or public health experts, and reflecting on what changed between your first version and your final one. That iterative story — the problems you ran into and how you solved them — is what will help others trust and adopt your tool. Your technical foundation is strong. Now build the human story around it to match.

round2_scores.comments_panel: The Agathon project is one of the most striking projects I’ve evaluated in the field of educational technology and the ethical use of artificial intelligence. By developing a “Socratic AI whiteboard” that provides step-by-step guidance rather than direct answers—thereby addressing the “ethical dilemma” created when students receive immediate answers from AI during challenging moments—the project absolutely deserves full marks under the Innovation and Responsible AI criteria. Including teachers in the design and establishing a system that allows them to monitor how dependent students are on AI has significantly strengthened the project’s impact. Additionally, realizing that their initially chosen AI model was too costly and rebuilding the application from scratch around a faster and more sustainable model is an excellent example of engineering and process management. I was unfortunately forced to deduct points solely under the ‘Social Issue and SDG Connection’ criterion; because, despite identifying an excellent problem in education, they made no reference to any of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) required by the evaluation guidelines. Despite this procedural oversight, this innovative vision, which champions academic integrity, certainly deserves to be showcased in the Second Round. While the app interface shown in the video differs from the actual one—there are some discrepancies—I still consider it a project worth listening to.
round2_scores.comments_student:
Dear Luca and Rushil, I would like to sincerely congratulate you on the Agathon project you developed to address a fundamental issue in education! This collaborative effort, born from the combination of Luca’s 8th-grade mentoring experience and Rushil’s software expertise on a fantastic project like “Boba Drops,” is truly inspiring. By eliminating the dilemma of students using AI as a mere “answer machine” and designing an “AI whiteboard” that poses questions using the Socratic method, you’ve achieved a tremendous innovation in the ethical use of technology in education. Recognizing that your initial AI model was costly and slow during the design process, and deciding to rebuild the application from scratch around a faster, more scalable model, demonstrates that you possess the vision of a real-world professional software developer. ISTE26AI109.ai_tools_used:
Research and Synthesis with Microsoft Copilot
The project began with an intentional use of Microsoft Copilot to bridge the gap between complex medical concepts and student understanding. As an AI-powered research assistant, Copilot allowed the team to gather and synthesize extensive data regarding spinal anatomy and specific deformities like scoliosis, kyphosis, and lordosis. This tool was chosen because it could quickly organize vast amounts of information into clear, actionable takeaways that served as the foundation for the projectâ€™s educational components. To ensure accuracy and maintain human oversightâ€”a key requirement of the challengeâ€”the students did not rely on the AI alone;they validated every piece of information through a professional medical lecture and a hands-on Q&A session with a doctor at KeÅ¾ovica Spa, ensuring the data used was medically sound.

Motion Tracking via Micro:bit Machine Learning
The core technical innovation of the project involved training machine learning models to recognize physical movement patterns using Micro:bit technology. By utilizing built-in accelerometer and gyroscope sensors, the team collected real-time data samples of themselves performing various exercises both correctly and incorrectly. This AI-driven approach was essential because traditional "if-then" coding is often too rigid to account for the natural variance in human movement. Through iterative testing, the students acted as the primary "human-in-the-loop," constantly refining the training parameters and adjusting sensor sensitivity whenever the AI failed to correctly identify a movement. This process turned a simple hardware device into a smart rehabilitation tool capable of providing immediate corrective feedback to help peers perform exercises correctly.

Personalized Interaction through PictoBlox
The AI SpineCare App, developed using PictoBlox, integrated sophisticated AI features to enhance user engagement and data security. The app utilized Face Recognition technology to identify and log in registered users, ensuring that progress tracking remained private and personalized to each individual. Beyond identification, the app featured a logic-based AI system that processed incoming data from the exercise devices to generate tailored recommendations and motivational "summative reports". By choosing AI for this interface, the team moved beyond a "one-size-fits-all" solution, creating a digital environment that adapts to the specific needs and performance levels of each student user.

Ethical Communication
Throughout this process, the team remained mindful of ethical implications, focusing on user privacy during face registration and emphasizing that their tools are meant to assist, not replace, professional healthcare providers. By combining these various AI applications, the team demonstrated a creative and responsible use of technology to solve a pressing community health problem.

round2_scores.comments_panel:
This team identified a real, documented health crisis in their own community and built something to address it. The local health report data, the visit to Kežovica Spa, the conversation with a rehabilitation specialist, these are not things most student teams do. They went out, talked to people, and let what they found shape what they built. That foundation makes the whole project credible.
The Micro:bit hardware system is the standout piece. Four physical devices, each targeting a different area of the spine, communicating via radio signals to a central data table — that is a real system architecture. The ML model training using accelerometer data to distinguish correct from incorrect movement is technically sound and genuinely innovative at this level. The human-in-the-loop approach to bias reduction, training on diverse body types, shows they thought carefully about who would actually use this.
The PictoBlox application is where the project falls short of its own ambitions. What is presented in the brochure as an AI-powered personalized feedback system is, in practice, a block-based interface that displays exercise photos and plays audio descriptions. The face recognition login is a nice touch but the core interaction is simpler than the documentation suggests. There is also a bug where the say and speak blocks fire simultaneously, creating overlapping audio — something that should have been caught in testing. The application layer reads as middle school level compared to the sophistication of the hardware work.
The 10-day longitudinal study and the 40-student survey are real evidence of impact. Not many teams produce data like this. Recommended for the finalist stage.
round2_scores.comments_student:
You started this project the right way. You looked at your community, found a real problem backed by actual health data, and went and talked to a doctor about it. That matters more than most teams realize. A lot of projects start with a solution and work backward. You started with the people who are affected.
The physical devices are impressive. Training a machine learning model to recognize correct and incorrect exercise form using Micro:bit sensors, then linking four separate devices to a central data table via radio, that is not a simple thing to build. The fact that it works, and that you have 10 days of data showing improvement across three students, means you built something real.
Be honest about the app, though. The PictoBlox side of the project is much simpler than your brochure describes. Showing exercise photos and playing audio instructions is useful, but it is not the same as AI-driven personalized recommendations. There is also a sound bug, the text and voice outputs play at the same time and overlap. Fix that before your next demonstration. When you present this, show the hardware. That is your strongest work and it should be front and center.
One more thing: your pitch video has audio but it does not show the system working live. A 30-second clip of someone wearing the device, doing an exercise, and seeing the Micro:bit respond would be more convincing than any amount of text on screen. You have something worth showing. Show it properly.

scores.comments_panel: ASAP Pitches converts student research into slide decks using OpenAI and Claude. The ethical design intentions are clear: no data stored, user input only, word count limits to discourage misuse. Feedback was gathered from DECA and HOSA students and at least one documented change was made based on that feedback. The concept itself is not new but the deliberate responsible use constraints show real design thinking. Testing comfirmed that the tool handles multilingual input correctly and produces output in the same language as the input, which is a genuine usability strength not mentioned in the pitch. However, testing at higher slide counts exposed a significant reliability problem. When the requested slide count exceeds what the input content can reasonably support, the system begins repeating ideas across multiple slides, padding thin content, and generating slides on topics entirely absent from the original input. This is hallucination in a practical sense and directly contradicts the team's claim that the tool only processes information users directly input. The core use case at a reasonable slide count works well, but this failure mode is a design quality concern the team needs to address before wider deployment.
scores.comments_student: You built something real, tested it with actual users and improved it based on what they told you. Your explanation of the privacy and bias decisions was specific and honest, and it was clear you understood why those choices mattered, not just that they were required. One thing worth knowing: the tool also works in other languages and produces output matching the input language, which is a strength you did not mention but users will notice. The area for improvement is what happens when someone requests more slides than their research can support. In testing, the system started repeating the same ideas across different slides, stretching thin content, and eventually adding slides on topics that were never in the original notes at all. That last point matters because your pitch says the tool only uses what the student inputs, and that is not always true. Putting a cap on slide count relative to input length, or flagging when the content is too short for the requested number of slides, would fix this and make your responsible use argument fully accurate. Speaker notes are included which is good, but they are as brief as the bullet points. If they explained the reasoning behind each point or suggested how to expand verbally, the tool would move from a starting point to a real learning scaffold.
The link to the prototype you provided https://asa-ppitch.vercel.app/success leads to a page for downloading the deck. A bit confusing for the end client.

round2_scores.comments_panel: Although the written description of the ASAP Pitches project is brief, the team’s presentation video highlights the project’s true technical and visionary strength. The problem the students identified based on their own DECA club experiences is brilliantly supported in the video by the emphasis on school and education (SDG 4: Quality Education), securing a perfect score for the “Social Issue” criterion. In particular, the explanation of the algorithm running in the background, the description of how different programs were integrated, and the display of coding snippets on screen provide the strong visual evidence required for the “Design Quality” and “Innovation” criteria. The team’s management of the process by working with an expert mentor is highly successful in terms of collaboration. While ethical details such as AI’s data privacy were addressed somewhat superficially, this is a very strong student project that deserves a spot in Round 2 (Website Highlights) due to its algorithm design and training objectives. Although the explanations were brief, I clearly understood there was more to it upon reviewing the video. It is important to also articulate this in writing.
round2_scores.comments_student: You did an amazing job, Sahasra and Alana! Drawing on your experiences in the DECA club, developing ASAP Pitches to solve the challenging “first draft” problem that many students face is a tremendous display of empathy. Your presentation video was particularly impressive; by showcasing the algorithm behind your project, how you integrated different programs, and your coding processes, you demonstrated just how strong your technical design skills (design thinking) are. Additionally, your emphasis on the challenges of school presentations and education in the video perfectly conveyed your project’s global goal (SDG 4: Quality Education) to the jury. Including an expert mentor like Ms. Brent in your project development process and working with her feedback was very professional. If you include details on how your AI model protects student data and privacy in future updates, your project will be truly unbeatable. I sincerely congratulate you on this innovative and technical algorithm that makes students’ lives easier!

scores.comments_student:
The project presents a meaningful idea; however, there are several concerns that limit its readiness for advancement to Round 2.

First, the focus of the solution lacks clarity and consistency. At the beginning of the video, the problem is framed around math support, which leads to the expectation of a subject-specific tutoring tool. Later, the scope shifts to all subjects, without a clear explanation of how the tool effectively supports this broader range. This inconsistency weakens the overall direction of the project.

Second, the innovation is limited. The proposed solution closely resembles existing free AI tools that are already widely accessible (e.g., platforms requiring only an email login). The project does not clearly explain what differentiates this tool from current solutions or how it offers added value beyond what already exists.

Third, there are significant concerns regarding responsible AI use. The tool appears to require users to input personal information such as full name, grade level, and study details, yet there is no clear mention of data privacy, protection, or usage policies.

Additionally, the project does not address a critical aspect of AI usage:
there is no warning about potential inaccuracies (AI hallucinations) or guidance encouraging students to verify information with teachers or reliable sources. Given that the target audience includes students who may not be familiar with proper AI use, this omission is a serious limitation.

Overall, while the intention is positive, the project currently lacks clarity, differentiation, and responsible implementation measures, which are essential for progression to the next round.

round2_scores.comments_student:
What you did well: Your team demonstrated exceptional collaboration and a beautifully clear division of labor! Having Conner as the ideator, Arriana as the programmer, and Maezy as the storyteller is a highly professional way to tackle a complex project. Furthermore, finding your inspiration right in your hometown of Maumee, Ohio, by wanting to support local favorites like Mama C's donuts, gave your project incredible heart and a clear, authentic purpose. We also loved the thoughtful additions to your Google Site, such as the language translation button and the PDF export feature, which show you were thinking carefully about user accessibility.

Area for Growth (The Prototype): While your bot does a good job staying on the topic of business, its responses are currently very generalized. To take an AI tool to the next level, it needs to provide something a user couldn't easily get by just typing a question into a standard AI like Gemini or ChatGPT. In the future, try exploring ways to "ground" your bot in specific, local data—such as feeding it documents about Ohio small business grants, local zoning laws, or regional marketing strategies so that it gives highly specific, tailored advice to the exact businesses you want to help.

Another Area for Growth (Testing and Iteration): You identified a fantastic target audience: local small business owners. The most important next step in your design process is to actually put your prototype in their hands! Taking Business Bot to the owners of Mama C's or other local shops, asking them to test it, and then improving the bot based on their direct feedback is what turns a great classroom prototype into a powerful real-world tool. We are so proud of the teamwork you built and the new skills you developed during this challenge

scores.comments_panel:
Digital Product Testing: I highly recommend thoroughly testing the actual digital product link. While the submission essay claims the app uses "AI-powered speech analysis" to provide personalized feedback, my hands-on testing revealed this feature is currently non-functional. The app does not actually process or listen to the user's audio input; it simply operates on a loop that provides generic, positive feedback regardless of what noise is made. Pitch Video Pacing: I scored Presentation & Communication a 2. To fit within the required time limit, the student artificially sped up the audio of the pitch video. This resulted in a rushed, unnatural delivery that required me to constantly pause the video just to comprehend the information, failing the rubric's requirement that the narrative be "easy to understand for a broad audience."

Strong Community Connection: Despite the technical and presentation flaws, the student's foundation is excellent. The project stems from authentic volunteer experience and directly addresses a documented shortage of tutors for neurodiverse children.
scores.comments_student:
What you did well: Your dedication to supporting neurodiverse learners is incredibly inspiring. Drawing from your three years of volunteer experience to address the tutor shortage shows a profound connection to your community. Furthermore, your user research is outstanding—surveying parents and hosting an intro seminar with over 60 families proves that you are actively listening to the people you want to help. The user interface you designed is also very clean, calming, and visually accessible for your target audience.

Areas for Growth (The Prototype): Your essay mentions that ChatBuddy utilizes "AI-powered speech analysis" and "adaptive AI models that respond to each child’s speech patterns". However, during testing, the app did not actually process audio input and instead provided generic positive feedback regardless of what was said. In future iterations, it is vital to actually integrate the backend AI listening models you described, or be completely transparent in your pitch about what features are fully functional versus what is currently a "mock-up." This is especially critical because your app features a parent dashboard that tracks and reports on the child's milestones and accuracy. Providing fabricated progress metrics to caregivers breaks trust and severely undermines the app's goal of making communication practice "measurable". True AI literacy requires transparency about a tool's current limitations.

Areas for Growth (The Pitch): You have so much great information to share, but artificially speeding up the audio in your pitch video to meet the time limit made it very difficult to digest your message. When an audience has to constantly pause a video to understand the speaker, the impact of the story is lost. In the future, focus on editing down your script to highlight only the most critical points so you can speak at a natural, engaging pace.

ISTE26AI123.ai_tools_used:
AI Tools Used for Cogito:

1. mlburnham/Political_DEBATE_large_v1.0 (Hugging Face)

Purpose: First-stage classifier that determines whether input text contains political content
Model Type: DeBERTa (Decoding-enhanced BERT with disentangled attention) fine-tuned on political discourse datasets
Accuracy: 92% on political content detection
How It's Used: When a user submits an article URL via Cogito's Bias Detector, this model analyzes whether the content is political. Non-political content is flagged as such;political content proceeds to the second classifier.
Training Data: Trained on 12 datasets related to political partisanship and discourse
Transparency Feature: Users can enable "detailed analysis mode" to see a line-by-line breakdown showing which text segments influenced the model's decision

2. matous-volf/political-leaning-deberta-large (Hugging Face)

Purpose: Second-stage classifier that determines political leaning of confirmed political text
Model Type: DeBERTa-large fine-tuned specifically for political bias classification
Accuracy: 89% on left/right/neutral classification
How It's Used: After text is confirmed as political, this model classifies it as left-leaning, right-leaning, or relatively neutral
Output: Provides both a classification label and a confidence score on a scale from -1.0 (left) to +1.0 (right), with values near zero indicating neutral content
Transparency Feature: Line-by-line breakdown highlights specific phrases and word choices that contributed to the classification, helping users understand the model's reasoning
Integration: Results are displayed visually with color-coded indicators (blue for left, red for right, gray for neutral) alongside the detailed analysis

3. Hugging Face Spaces

Purpose: Cloud infrastructure for running AI model inference
How It's Used: Both bias detection models are hosted on Hugging Face Spaces, allowing Cogito's Flask backend to make API calls for real-time article analysis
Technical Implementation: Cogito's server sends article text to the Hugging Face Spaces endpoint, receives the classification result, and displays it to users with full transparency about how the decision was reached

round2_scores.comments_panel:
CrisisIQ is one of the most technically ambitious projects in this batch. The team built a full-stack disaster response platform with a native Android app in Kotlin/Compose, a Python FastAPI backend deployed on AWS, a web-based command center dashboard, and two distinct AI models: YOLOv8 for disaster image classification and Mistral (running locally via Ollama) as an emergency guidance chatbot. The 10-page system documentation describes real engineering decisions (coroutine management, SSE streaming, manual override of AI classifications) at a level that would be impressive from a college team, let alone high school sophomores. The live server was confirmed accessible during review.
I am recommending this project for finals, but want to flag two areas of hesitation for the panel. First, the pitch video runs 3:47 (47 seconds over the time limit), with a notable portion spent on stock news footage rather than substance. Second, the Collaboration & Process criterion was the hardest to score. The Team Story reads as a project description rather than a reflection on collaboration, roles, iteration, or lessons learned. The 10-page documentation provides indirect evidence that significant collaboration occurred, but the team never tells that story. I scored Criterion 6 as a 3, giving credit for what the documentation implies, but the panel should be aware that the explicit reflection the rubric asks for is largely absent.
The project earns its Innovation & Creativity score of 4 decisively. The multi-stakeholder architecture (citizen reporter, command center operator, responder), the purposeful use of two AI models for distinct roles, and the cloud-deployed full-stack system demonstrate exceptional creative problem-solving.
round2_scores.comments_student:
CrisisIQ is technically outstanding. Building a native Android app, a Python backend with two AI models, and a live web dashboard deployed on AWS is a remarkable achievement, and your 10-page system documentation shows a level of engineering maturity that sets your project apart. The multi-stakeholder design (citizen reporters, command center, responders) reflects real understanding of how emergency response coordination works, and your decision to allow manual override of AI disaster classifications shows smart thinking about the limits of automated systems.
Two areas to strengthen. First, your pitch video ran 47 seconds past the 3-minute limit. Time limits are strictly judged in competitions, and the stock news footage in the opening, while cinematic, consumed time you could have used for the substance judges care about most: your process, your ethical considerations, and your SDG connection. For future pitches, lead with your own demo footage and your own voices, because the product is strong enough to speak for itself. Second, your Team Story should tell the story of your collaboration: who took which role, what challenges you faced, how you iterated on the design, and whether you consulted with emergency response professionals. The rubric values process reflection as much as the final product, and your Team Story currently reads more as a project description than a narrative of your journey. Given the sophistication of what you built, there is clearly a great collaboration story to tell.
Keep building CrisisIQ. The foundation you have is genuinely impressive, and with stronger storytelling around your process and ethics, this project could stand out at the highest levels of competition.

ISTE26AI130.project_name: EcoBuddy: AI Climate & Sustainability Action Assistant

scores.comments_panel:
EcoBuddy shows some real ambitious thinking and the strategic research behind the concept is impressive for high school students. However, I want to flag two concerns for the panel's consideration before scores are finalized.

First, the submission deck that appears in the video is explicitly labeled 'Blue Ocean Strategy Competition' on both the title and closing slides. The entire presentation is structured around Blue Ocean Strategy frameworks (ERRC Grid, Strategy Canvas, Buyer Utility Map, Three Tiers of Noncustomers) rather than the ISTE AI Innovator's Challenge criteria. This appears to be a cross-submission, and I defer to the panel and program coordinators to determine whether this meets eligibility requirements.

Second, I have concerns about the authenticity of student voice in this submission. The link provided for the "Team Story" component leads directly to the same slide deck featured in the video, adding no additional context, reflection, or evidence of the students' process. There is no written narrative, no documentation of iteration, and no personal student perspective present anywhere in the submission materials. The pitch video itself contains no student voices — narration is fully AI-generated, which is not prohibited, but in the absence of any other evidence of authentic student ownership, it compounds the concern. Taken together, these factors make it difficult to assess the degree to which this work reflects genuine student voice and shared purpose, which is central to Criterion 6.

Neither concern has been used to explicitly penalize students on individual rubric criteria, as both are more appropriately handled at the administrative level. I am flagging them here so the panel can make an informed determination.
scores.comments_student:
EcoBuddy addresses a real and important problem, and your problem framing, backed by credible data from UNICEF, IEA, and UCL, is one of the strongest parts of this submission. The Learn → Act → Reflect loop is a creative framework, your market research is well-done, and the fact that you built a working Flutter app shows genuine follow-through.

The area most in need of growth is the connection between your pitch and your product. EcoBuddy is presented as an AI personalization engine, but the current implementation uses rule-based mission selection. For an AI-centered competition, judges will look closely at how AI is actually used.  Additionally, your submission does not address bias, privacy, or the limitations of your AI choices. Being precise and transparent about what your AI does and doesn't do will significantly strengthen future submissions.

We also wanted more evidence of your process (who you tested with, what feedback changed your direction, and how your thinking evolved). That story is just as important as the final product. Continue developing EcoBuddy with real user testing and honest AI documentation, and you will have a compelling submission.

ISTE26AI133.ai_tools_used:
As AI Innovators and creators of Egret Eye which promotes the responsible use of AI in social media, we explored and integrated multiple AI tools throughout the app creation process. Each tool played a significant role in ensuring that the app was informative, visually engaging, and easy to use. ChatGPT was the main research and content creation assistant. We used it to generate educational content such as stories, poems, and digital citizenship pledges about responsible AI use. We edited and improved the written content for clarity and engagement and learned and got help with coding using Thunkable, including understanding blocks, logic, and building specific app features. We also used Magic School AI for creative prompts for stories and social media scenarios. Also, some of the AI-generated questions and quizzes about digital responsibility were done here. Canva AI was our go-to design tool. We designed the user interface (UI) and prototype the app layout using Canva AI. We also generated AI-enhanced images for app backgrounds and icons. Thunkable was the platform we used to build the actual app. It has an AI-assisted template generator that helped us get ideas on user interface layouts and features. We also structured the app with proper navigation and visual elements. All logos, icons, and illustrations used in the app were created using AI-powered design tools.Visuals were later reviewed and enhanced using Canva and integrated into the Thunkable interface.
	
By combining all these tools, we were able to design, develop, and prototype an AI-powered app that educates users, especially students, on how to use social media responsibly in the age of artificial intelligence.

round2_scores.comments_panel:
This team provided some of the most robust evidence of user testing in the entire competition. They gathered feedback from international students, teachers, and parents and used that data to redesign their "Old Version" into the highly polished "New Version".

Rather than just using AI to build a tool, they built a tool about the ethical use of AI. Their "AI Reality Check" quiz and multilingual accessibility features prove they have a profound understanding of what it means to be responsible digital citizens.

Noted Technical Limitations (For Future Consideration): While the educational framework is outstanding, testing the app revealed that the AI backend relies on a model with a knowledge cutoff of 2023. This is highly problematic for a social media truth-seeking tool, which requires the ability to cross-reference live, real-time data to debunk current events. Furthermore, the industry is increasingly finding that automated "AI text detectors" are notoriously unreliable and prone to false positives. Despite these technical limitations of their chosen API, their strong focus on digital literacy and education makes this a standout project, and I still give it my highest recommendation for advancement to Round 3.
round2_scores.comments_student:
What you did well: Your project is an absolute triumph! Your team demonstrated a brilliant division of labor between Archi, Jun, and Andrew, and your ability to weave together multiple AI tools (ChatGPT, Magic School AI, Canva AI, and Thunkable) into one seamless application is incredibly impressive. We were blown away by your dedication to accessibility and inclusion. Adding text-to-speech and multilingual support (English, Chinese, and Spanish) proves that you truly understand user-centered design. Furthermore, grounding your work in the UNESCO AI Ethics framework and gathering feedback from teachers and students shows a highly professional development process. You should be incredibly proud!

Areas for Growth (Live Data Integration): During our testing of your "AI Truth Scan" feature, we noticed that the AI stated its knowledge was only current up to 2023. Because social media moves at lightning speed, a truth-seeking app needs to be able to analyze current events! In your next iteration, look into integrating a "live search" or web-browsing API into your Thunkable backend. This will allow your virtual assistant, EVA, to search the live internet to verify if a breaking news story is actually true.

Areas for Growth (The Limits of Detection): As you plan your future improvements ,like adding photo and video analysis, keep in mind that automated AI content detectors (for both text and images) are often incorrect and can falsely flag real human content as AI-generated. Because of this, continue leaning heavily into the educational side of your app. Teach your users how to spot fake news and deepfakes themselves, rather than just relying on the AI Truth Scan to give them a definitive answer.

Keep up the outstanding work. You are well on your way to becoming leaders in the tech industry!

scores.comments_panel: Find Your Home addresses a real and specific problem: immigrants and first-time buyers navigating U.S. housing eligibility without understanding the terminology or the process. The personal connection is genuine and the app is deployed and testable. The financial triage approach, AMI explanation feature, and downloadable personalized guide go beyond what mainstream property platforms offer. However, several concerns affect the score. The team claims the app does not collect sensitive data, but the system requires income, household size, and location to function. These are not in the same category as SSN or bank account numbers, but the blanket no sensitive data framing is inaccurate. Language support is described as covering languages from all over the world, which overstates actual coverage. The mobile interface has a documented usability problem where prior questions scroll off screen during a conversation, which matters significantly for a tool aimed at users who may primarily access it on a phone. The submission does not state how many users were involved in testing or provide any estimate of how many people the team aims to reach, which makes the impact and longevity case harder to assess.
scores.comments_student: Arian and Sahiti, the eligibility matching and AMI explanation are the strongest parts of the app because they solve something mainstream platforms skip entirely. The language ordering based on real U.S. usage patterns shows genuine thought. Three things are worth fixing. First, the safety notice says not to share sensitive data but the app then asks for income and household details. Be more precise: tell users exactly what is used only within the session and what is not stored. Second, the mobile experience needs work. When previous messages scroll off screen during a conversation, following the flow becomes hard, and your target users are likely on phones. Third, your impact story needs numbers. How many people did you test with? Who were they? How many users are you realistically trying to reach in the next year? Even rough estimates make the case for your project much stronger. For keeping the app running, look closer to home first: your school district, a local library, or a community center that serves immigrant families could help you reach real users without needing a large organization behind you.

team_story.value: Wildfires are an escalating environmental and societal threat, damaging ecosystems, degrading air quality, and endangering communities and infrastructure. Existing wildfire analytics often focus on static risk scores or short-horizon simulations, but they donâ€™t capture how a fireâ€™s behavior changes over time (e.g., persisting, escalating, or collapsing) under shifting terrain and weather conditions. Our project addresses this gap by modeling wildfire progression as a dynamic, interpretable process to better anticipate dangerous regime shifts and support earlier, better-informed response. Our proposed solution is FireCast: a terrain-aware, state-space AI framework that models wildfire progression as transitions between latent â€œbehavior statesâ€ (e.g., stable, moderate growth, extreme growth, rain-driven collapse) rather than only producing static risk scores, a model we have not only tested thoroughly ourselves but also have hosted as a proof-of-concept on a website.

It helps analyze user input, give helpful suggestions, and improve interaction within the app. By using Gemini AI, Flourished becomes more efficient, user-friendly, and capable of supporting users with smarter features. ðŸŒ±âœ¨

scores.comments_panel:
While Flourished presents a meaningful and engaging concept supported by strong teamwork, the project does not currently demonstrate sufficient depth to progress to Round 2.

The team identifies a relevant issue—students’ disconnection from nature—and connects it to SDG 15: Life on Land. However, the problem remains broad and not clearly grounded in a specific community or measurable need, which limits its impact.

The proposed solution, a plant scanner application, shows creative intent, but lacks clear differentiation from existing tools and does not explain how it offers added value.

There is also limited evidence of design development and validation. While challenges are mentioned, the final product appears to have faced functionality issues, and there is no clear evidence of testing, user feedback, or iteration. The demonstration was unclear, particularly during the game component, where responses were shown without clearly defined questions, making user interaction difficult to follow.

In addition, the user experience lacks depth and longevity. Once users complete the available steps and activities, there is no clear pathway for continued engagement or progression. The experience appears limited to its initial programmed interactions, which may lead to reduced user interest over time.

A key limitation is the absence of responsible AI considerations. The project does not address data privacy, accuracy, bias, or the possibility of AI errors, nor does it encourage users to verify information or explore beyond the tool, which is especially important for student users.

While the team demonstrates strong collaboration, reflection, and commitment, the project lacks the technical clarity, differentiation, validation, and sustainability required for advancement.
scores.comments_student:
Your project, Flourished, presents a thoughtful and meaningful idea, and it is clear that your team demonstrated strong collaboration, persistence, and genuine passion throughout the process. The connection to SDG 15: Life on Land is relevant, and your motivation to raise awareness about nature among students is commendable.

To strengthen your project further, consider clarifying the focus of your solution and how it specifically addresses a defined group or community. It would also be helpful to highlight what makes your app unique compared to existing tools and how it provides added value.

In addition, developing the project further through user testing, feedback, and clearer demonstration of how the app works would improve its overall effectiveness. Expanding the experience to include ongoing engagement for users could also enhance its long-term impact.

Finally, as your project involves AI, it would be important to include guidance for users on responsible use, such as encouraging them to verify information and be aware of possible inaccuracies.

Overall, your teamwork, resilience, and commitment are clear strengths. With further development and refinement, your idea has the potential to grow into a more impactful and well-defined solution.

We used ChatGPT and Google Gemini to help us brainstorm ideas, refine explanations of technical concepts, and troubleshoot parts of our heuristic detection framework. Importantly, we made the design decisions ourselvesâ€”AI tools helped us iterate faster, but our team evaluated the suggestions, tested the outputs, and determined how the final system should function. round2_scores.comments_panel: The Hoʻoia project is one of the most philosophical and mature works I have evaluated so far in terms of the criteria of “Responsible and Ethical Use of Artificial Intelligence” and “Innovation and Creativity.” The team’s decision to move away from creating a run-of-the-mill “true or false” black-box AI detection tool and instead adopt the concept of “Explainable AI” (XAI)—which explains to users why something is suspicious and supports human judgment—is a remarkable example of design-driven thinking. Using tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Lovable not as decision-makers but solely as “assistive collaborators” during the development process is a textbook example of ethical AI usage. Additionally, their inclusion of a clear privacy statement in their system and their use of various visual datasets to prevent bias is highly commendable. I had to deduct points in just one criterion, the ‘Social Issue and SDG Connection’ section; because although they identified the social issue very well by conducting excellent interviews with the elderly (kupuna) and young people, they did not link their project to any United Nations Sustainable Development Goal (SDG). Despite this minor shortcoming, it is an exceptional project that absolutely deserves a spot in Round 2 (Website Highlights) for the transparent and educational vision it brings to digital literacy. During the review process, this project stood out, but I noticed that no information was shared about the algorithm or the coding used in its development.
round2_scores.comments_student: Dear Skyler, Johnray, and Dylan; I sincerely congratulate all of you for designing such a thoughtful, transparent, and educational project! Starting your project by conducting interviews directly with the elderly and young people—in other words, by listening to your community—is a wonderful example of empathy. Instead of creating a run-of-the-mill diagnostic tool to address the confusion people feel in the online world, your idea of building an “Explainable AI” that explains signals like edge sharpness, noise variance, and color uniformity to users is, in a word, magnificent. Using ChatGPT, Gemini, and Lovable not as tools that fully control the system, but merely as ‘assistant tools’ to accelerate your ideas, demonstrates just how high your understanding of Responsible and Ethical AI is. As you put it, your conclusion that AI is not just a tool that ‘provides answers, but supports human understanding’ is a very mature vision. I have just one piece of advice to take your project to an even more unbeatable level in the future: Clearly state in your texts which of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (e.g., SDG 4: Quality Education) your wonderful digital literacy vision serves. This is the wonderful vision you’ve demonstrated by saying, “This is not a finished solution, but a starting point.” Additionally, the code and algorithm behind this project will serve as a positive reason to highlight your work. Best of luck.

round2_scores.comments_panel:
This project absolutely deserves to be a Website Highlight and advance to Round 3. It demonstrates a maturity regarding AI limitations that is rare even among professional developers.

When I initially tested this app using real and AI-generated photos, the results were highly inaccurate. However, reading the team's documentation reveals that they already know this, and it is the entire thesis of their project! They explicitly state that their testing revealed "visual artifacts alone cannot guarantee certainty." 

Focus on Media Literacy: Instead of trying to force a flawed AI to be perfectly accurate, they pivoted to use the AI as a teaching tool. The goal of Ho'oia is not to replace human judgment, but to be transparent about its own uncertainty to force users to engage critically with digital media.  This level of ethical foresight and understanding of AI's fundamental limitations is exactly what this challenge aims to promote. I highly recommend advancing this project.
round2_scores.comments_student:
What you did well: Your project is an absolute masterclass in critical thinking and the ethical application of AI! Many developers would have tested their AI detector, realized it was inaccurate, and given up. Instead, your team had the profound maturity to realize that the inaccuracy wasn't a failure, it was a feature. By pivoting your project to focus on media literacy, transparency, and teaching humans how to spot digital artifacts, you addressed the root cause of the problem: a lack of trust and education in the digital age.  Your pitch video was also incredibly professional and engaging; breaking down the fake whale video was a perfect way to demonstrate the need for your tool. Furthermore, ensuring the tool runs entirely locally in the browser to protect user privacy is a fantastic design choice.

Areas for Growth: Your UI is very clean, but some of the raw data (like "Image Entropy" or "Luminance Distribution") might still be a bit overwhelming for the older generation (the kupuna) you interviewed.  In future iterations, consider adding a simplified "tutorial mode" or plain-English tooltips next to those complex terms so that users of all tech-literacy levels can fully understand the heuristics you are teaching them. You have built something incredibly thoughtful and necessary for today's world. Keep up the phenomenal work!

ISTE26AI149.ai_tools_used: Mediapipe, CNN, LSTM, TensorFlow Lite, OpenCV: MediaPipe is an artificial intelligence framework used for hand detection and tracking. It identifies and tracks 21 key landmarks on the hand, such as the fingers and palm joints. Instead of analyzing the whole image, MediaPipe converts the hand into numerical coordinates, which makes gesture recognition faster and more accurate. These landmark points are then used as input for the machine learning models that interpret the sign language gestures. Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) is a deep learning model designed to recognize patterns in images. In the system, the CNN analyzes the shape and structure of the hand gestures captured by the camera. It is particularly effective for identifying static signs, such as letters in the Filipino Sign Language (FSL) alphabet. The model automatically learns visual features from training data, allowing it to classify gestures without manual feature programming. Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is a type of recurrent neural network used for analyzing sequences and motion over time. In this project, LSTM helps recognize dynamic gestures, or signs that involve movement rather than a single hand shape. By remembering previous frames in a video sequence, LSTM can understand how a gesture changes over time and improve the accuracy of sign recognition.TensorFlow Lite is a lightweight version of the TensorFlow machine learning framework used to run trained AI models efficiently on embedded devices. In the system, it allows the CNN and LSTM models to perform real-time gesture recognition while using minimal processing power, making it suitable for hardware such as the ESP32-CAM. OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library) is used for image processing and camera handling. It captures frames from the webcam or camera module and performs preprocessing tasks such as grayscale conversion, noise reduction, and image resizing. This prepares the visual data so the AI models can analyze it more accurately.

round2_scores.comments_panel: The iHear project is one of the strongest projects I’ve evaluated in terms of hardware and software integration. This team has established a flawless connection to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs); they have directly linked the project to SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-being) and SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities). Instead of designing expensive smart glasses from the start, they produced a cost-effective and accessible physical prototype using an ESP32 camera and an Arduino Uno, which fully deserves top marks in the ‘Design Quality’ and ‘Innovation’ criteria. Adding three physical mode buttons to the device that allow switching between numbers, letters, and words to prevent the AI from confusing similar symbols and to improve translation accuracy is an excellent example of analytical problem-solving. Additionally, the task distribution among the 6-person team (coding, design, documentation) is very clearly documented and demonstrates strong teamwork. Under the ‘Responsible and Ethical AI’ criterion, they only need to provide a bit more detail regarding the privacy of camera data; however, overall, this is a project with a very high social impact that more than deserves to advance to Round 2 (Website Highlights).

round2_scores.comments_panel: This team's engineering capabilities are genuinely outstanding. Integrating MediaPipe, CNN, and LSTM models onto an ESP32-CAM and Arduino to run TensorFlow Lite locally is a highly advanced technical feat for students that demonstrates a deep understanding of edge computing and computer vision. Despite this technical brilliance, I had to score Ethical Use of AI and Design Quality & Accessibility as 2s. Based on the submitted narrative and prototype images, there is no evidence of user testing with the deaf community, which is a critical requirement for a communication accessibility tool under the "Design Quality" rubric. Furthermore, there is no discussion of how they are mitigating inherent computer vision biases, such as performance across different skin tones or lighting conditions, or handling privacy concerns with a camera-based device. Because Round 2 requires a minimum total score of 24 and dictates that no single criterion may be rated as a 2, this project unfortunately does not qualify to advance as a Website Highlight. The lack of user testing and ethical foresight holds back an otherwise incredible piece of engineering.
round2_scores.comments_student: Your technical and engineering skills are absolutely phenomenal! Your goal to bridge the communication gap between the deaf and hearing communities using Filipino Sign Language is a deeply meaningful application of technology, and you have built a remarkably strong technical foundation. As you look toward the next steps, we highly encourage you to focus on user-centered design. Hardware and code are only half the journey; the other half is ensuring the tool actually works seamlessly for the people who need it. Additionally, camera-based AI systems come with unique ethical challenges. In future presentations, be sure to document how you are addressing privacy (such as whether the video feed stays completely on the device) and bias (such as ensuring the model is trained to accurately recognize hands of all skin tones and in varying lighting conditions). Factoring in these user testing and ethical considerations will elevate your project from a fantastic prototype to a truly responsible, real-world product. Keep up the amazing work!

ISTE26AI156.ai_tools_used: During the development of our project, we used several AI tools as creative and technical collaborators to support research, prototyping, and problem-solving. We used ChatGPT to brainstorm ideas, refine our problem statement, and help us explore possible indicators that could signal AI-generated images. ChatGPT also assisted with debugging code and helping us explain technical concepts in clear language for users. We used Google Gemini to conduct additional research, compare different approaches to AI image detection, and validate our design ideas by generating alternative perspectives and suggestions. scores.comments_panel:
Legatto is one of the stronger submissions in this round and merits serious consideration for advancement. These students built a genuinely functional, publicly deployed web application that substantially matches their pitch claims, a distinction worth noting given the variability across submissions in this category.

team_story.value:
Building a Friend in Your Pocket: My Journey Creating a Mental Wellness Companion

I created this app because I saw a problem that kept me up at night: one in five teens experiences a mental health challenge, yet so many never seek help due to stigma, cost, or simply not knowing where to turn. I wanted to build something that could be there for young people when they needed it most: a digital companion that offers support without judgment, available 24 hours a day.

I'm not trying to replace therapists. My app can't diagnose depression or prescribe medication. But what it can do is be the friend who listens at 2 a.m. when anxiety keeps someone awake, the supportive voice when academic stress feels crushing, or the gentle guide that helps someone find the courage to seek professional help when they need it most.

My purpose in creating this was simple yet profound: to normalize conversations about mental health and make emotional support accessible to every teen and young adult who needs it. The statistics that drove me to build this are sobering. Suicide is the second leading cause of death among young people aged 10 to 24, according to the CDC. Yet research from organizations like The Jed Foundation shows that when young people have access to support systems and coping strategies, outcomes improve dramatically. I wanted to be part of that solution.

When someone opens my app, I wanted them to feel welcomed, not overwhelmed. Instead of clinical questionnaires or intimidating forms, users are greeted with genuine warmth: "How are you feeling today?" I designed the app to adapt its tone based on their response. It's gentle and validating for those struggling, upbeat and encouraging for those celebrating wins. Through thoughtful questions about sleep, stress, social connections, and self-care, my app helps users reflect on their mental wellness without making them feel like they're being analyzed.

What makes my creation different from simply googling "how to feel better" is the personalized, empathetic approach I built into every interaction. I programmed it to offer evidence-based coping strategies: breathing exercises for anxiety, grounding techniques for overwhelming emotions, journaling prompts for self-discovery. It validates feelings without minimizing them, using phrases like "It's okay to feel this way" and "That sounds really difficult," because I learned through my research that sometimes, being heard is the first step toward healing.

But I also built in transparency about limitations. My app regularly reminds users that it's a supportive tool, not a replacement for professional care. When conversations reveal serious concerns, it immediately provides resources: crisis hotlines like 988, school counselors, and platforms like BetterHelp and Talkspace. I designed it to detect when someone needs more than technology can offer and to guide them toward that help without shame or hesitation.

The impact I hope to achieve extends beyond individual conversations. By making mental health support feel normal and accessible, I want to help reduce the stigma that prevents so many young people from seeking help. My app is available when school counselors aren't, affordable when therapy seems out of reach, and private when sharing feels scary. In an era where mental health challenges among youth are rising, I'm contributing to a new frontier in wellness support: not replacing human connection and professional care, but complementing them.

I built this to be the first step, the midnight companion, the judgment-free space where young people can begin to understand their feelings and find their path toward healing. Because I believe everyone deserves to feel heard, supported, and hopeful about their mental health journey.

scores.comments_panel:
While NutriAI presents a strong and relevant concept addressing personalized nutrition and preventive healthcare, the project does not currently demonstrate sufficient clarity and completeness to progress to Round 2.

Although the idea is well-motivated, the problem and its connection to a specific community were not clearly articulated in the video, which made it difficult to fully understand the context and target users.

The project shows evidence of testing and a developed interface, which is a strength. However, there are key clarity issues in the presentation. The system includes two input methods (a machine and an app), but the purpose and relationship between them are not explained. This creates confusion regarding how the product is intended to be used and by whom.

Additionally, the opening of the video (first ~40 seconds) includes a still image with text in Hindi, which may contain important information but is not accessible to a wider audience, limiting understanding of the project.

A critical concern is the handling of sensitive medical data. The project involves personal health and nutritional information, yet there is no discussion of data privacy, user eligibility, or safeguards. It is also unclear who the intended users are (general public vs. supervised use), especially given the inclusion of a machine component.

Finally, while the concept is strong, there is limited attention to responsible AI use, including the possibility of inaccuracies and the need for users to verify recommendations, which is especially important in a health-related application.

Overall, the project demonstrates strong thinking and potential, but lacks the clarity, justification, and responsible implementation details required for advancement.
scores.comments_student:
Your project, NutriAI, presents a very strong and meaningful idea, and it is clear that you have put significant effort into developing a solution that addresses an important real-world issue. The focus on personalized nutrition and preventive health is highly relevant, and your use of both mathematical modeling and AI concepts is impressive.

One of the strengths of your project is that you were able to build and demonstrate a working system, including testing and different input methods. This shows strong initiative and technical ability.

To further strengthen your project, consider clarifying how the system is intended to be used. For example, you introduced both a machine-based input and an app, but their roles and connection were not fully explained. Making this clearer would help users better understand your design.

It would also be helpful to ensure that all parts of your presentation are accessible to a wider audience. For instance, including translations or explanations for any non-English text would allow all viewers to fully understand your work.

Since your project involves health and personal data, an important next step would be to include guidance on data privacy, user safety, and responsible use. This could involve explaining who should use the system, how data is handled, and encouraging users to verify recommendations when needed.

Overall, your project shows strong potential, creativity, and dedication. With clearer communication and additional attention to user experience and responsibility, your idea could develop into a very impactful solution.

ISTE26AI176.project_name: Project SARA: Sustainable Analysis & Recipe Assistant
ISTE26AI176.ai_tools_used: During the development of Project S.A.R.A., we strategically utilized AI across both our hardware and software layers to move from basic monitoring to proactive waste prevention. On the hardware side, we used AI to refine our C++ Arduino code, specifically for implementing the averaging algorithm that filters out electrical noise from the MQ-2 sensor and DHT11, ensuring the buzzer only triggers for legitimate threats. For the software and user interface, we used Wix to design a professional mission landing page and Bolt to rapidly prototype the responsive web dashboard that hosts our live sensor data. Finally, we integrated Gemini as the core intelligence of our "Recipe Assistant," which takes the real-time alerts from our sensors and processes them to generate creative, waste-reducing culinary solutions.

The concept demonstrates strong potential impact and scalability. While the idea is well developed, further clarity on the technical implementation, user testing, and ethical considerations would strengthen the overall evaluation. Overall, this is a well-conceived and impactful project with strong communication and purpose.

Areas for Growth (The Prototype and AI Ethics): When dealing with healthcare and medicine, user safety is the absolute highest priority. While your Teachable Machine scanner is fantastic, the "ReAi" chatbot introduces a major safety risk. Because AI chatbots can sometimes "hallucinate" or make up facts, giving users an open chat box to ask medical questions can be dangerous. You should train the AI so that it politely refuses to diagnose illnesses or prescribe treatments.

Areas for Growth (Collaboration): Moving forward with any healthcare-related projects, it is absolutely essential to thoroughly document your collaboration with certified medical professionals. Having your app rigorously tested and verified by real doctors or botanists and detailing that exact process in your written project story is what will elevate your creation from a fantastic classroom prototype into a trustworthy, real-world medical tool. You have built a deeply impactful application with incredible potential, and we are so incredibly proud of your hard work!

ISTE26AI181.ai_tools_used: Jong AI (created from Groq AI specially for ReSiklo)

scores.comments_panel:
ReSiklo is a strong submission warranting a clear recommendation for advancement to Round 2.

Direct examination of the submitted files confirms a genuine, feature-rich Android application. The team provided both the MIT App Inventor source file (ResikloAPP.aia, 25.7 MB) and a compiled, installable APK (ReSikloAPP.apk, 30.1 MB). The APK size and asset volume are consistent with everything demonstrated in the pitch video, including the multilingual interface, the EcoScan AI classifier, the Learning Hub infographics, the EcoQuiz, and the gamification system. This is not a mockup or a prototype — it is a distributable Android application.

The submission stands out across nearly every criterion. Four SDGs are named explicitly (SDG 12, 4, 13, 11) with the problem grounded in the team's specific school environment. The AI implementation (Jong AI, built on the Groq inference API) is a legitimate technical choice. Groq provides fast LLM inference for open-source models and is comparable in sophistication to API-backed chatbots in other submissions. The multilingual support across seven languages, the live camera-based waste classification feature, and the school-wide auditorium deployment to the full student body of Dominican School Manila are each individually notable. Together they make a compelling case.

The one gap worth flagging is that the submission does not explain the underlying AI model powering EcoScan or address limitations, bias, or data privacy considerations for its student user base. Criterion 3 is the only area where this submission falls short of its otherwise exceptional standard.
scores.comments_student:
Thank you for submitting ReSiklo. This is an exceptional student project. Your problem framing is specific, empathetic, and grounded in your own school community, and naming four SDGs explicitly shows a sophisticated understanding of how local action connects to global goals. The app itself is genuinely impressive: a live AI waste classifier, multilingual support across seven languages, a learning hub, an interactive quiz, a gamified reward system, and a real-world resource directory together form a product that goes well beyond what most student teams attempt. Presenting it to your entire school in a packed auditorium demonstrates real community impact, not just a contest entry.

The area with the most room to grow is the technical explanation of your AI. Judges want to understand what model powers EcoScan, how you verified its accuracy, what its limitations are, and how you considered data privacy for student users. Your written materials touch on responsible AI use in general terms, but specifics about Jong AI and your Groq implementation would make your AI literacy argument much stronger. 

ReSiklo reflects genuine innovation, teamwork, and commitment to your community. Keep building it.

round2_scores.comments_panel:
SAFElink demonstrates impressive technical ambition. The team built a physical IoT device with environmental sensors, a GSM module for SMS alerts, and OpenAI API integration, all running on an ESP microcontroller. The problem (unreliable disaster communication in the Philippines) is deeply authentic and personally rooted, and the decision to use SMS rather than internet-dependent channels reflects genuine empathy for their target users.
The project's primary weakness is the gap between technical execution and the rubric's non-technical criteria. No specific UN SDG is identified in any submitted material. AI ethics, bias, and data privacy are never addressed, which is especially concerning for a system delivering safety-critical advice to fishers and farmers. The Team Story is brief and generic, with no discussion of individual roles, iteration, community testing, or reflection on the process. No live website URL was submitted despite the pitch referencing a monitoring dashboard.
Innovation and Creativity earned the project's highest mark (4) because the hardware/IoT approach with two-way AI advisory via SMS is genuinely original. However, the underdeveloped SDG connection, absent ethics discussion, thin collaboration narrative, and missing live demo prevent the project from reaching the finalist threshold across the full rubric.
round2_scores.comments_student:
Congratulations on an impressive and ambitious project. Building a physical IoT device with real sensors, a GSM module, and AI integration is a significant technical achievement, and your decision to use SMS rather than internet-dependent channels shows genuine understanding of your community's needs. The live demonstrations of sensor data and AI-generated fishing advice on a phone were the most compelling moments of your pitch and clearly showed a working system. Your personal connection to disaster preparedness in the Philippines comes through strongly, and it's clear this project matters to you.
A few areas that would strengthen your submission for future competitions. First, always name and explain the specific UN SDG(s) your project connects to, and describe why that connection is meaningful. Second, because SAFElink delivers safety-critical advice, judges need to see that you've considered what happens when the AI gives incorrect guidance, how user phone numbers are stored and protected, and what biases might exist in the model's recommendations. Third, your Team Story would benefit from describing each member's role, any experts or community members you consulted, challenges you encountered, and how feedback shaped your design. Finally, if your project includes a website dashboard, make sure to submit a working link so judges can verify it.
You've built something that many professional teams would find challenging. Keep developing SAFElink, and invest in telling the full story of your process alongside the technology itself.

ISTE26AI184.project_name: SARA AI - Smart Adaptive Resource Assistant
ISTE26AI184.ai_tools_used:
The core intelligence of the system is powered by the Groq API using the Llama 3.3 70B large language model. This model generates personalised explanations, answers student questions, and assists with quizzes and study sessions.
For speech interaction, SARA AI uses the Web Speech API, which enables both speech recognition and text-to-speech. This allows students to interact with the system entirely through voice commands, making the platform accessible to visually impaired learners.
To process images such as exam timetables and syllabus documents, the system integrates Tesseract.js for Optical Character Recognition (OCR). This allows the AI to extract text from uploaded images and convert them into readable and teachable content.
Development of the application was done using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and the entire system runs directly in the browser without requiring installation.

round2_scores.comments_panel: This is technically the most ambitious submission in the batch. The students built a genuine full-stack application combining PHP, MySQL, Python, and YOLOv8 computer vision in 35 days. The faction system, leaderboard, and gamified mini-games show real engineering ambition and the concept of turning SDG education into an RPG-style experience is genuinely creative. However, the deployment strategy is the project's most significant weakness. Requiring users to install XAMPP, configure a local MySQL database, and set up a Windows Task Scheduler task is a development environment, not a working deployment. The web interface itself is thoughtfully designed and the written manual with the walkthrough video provide clear guidance, but accessibility breaks down entirely at the installation stage. Additionally, there is no discussion of privacy or ethical considerations for the camera feature, and the YOLOv8 model is inconsistently referenced as YOLOv26 in documentation. These issues prevented higher scores on accessibility and responsible AI use despite the strong technical effort visible in the codebase.
round2_scores.comments_student:
Geriemee, Jaden, and Julian, building a full-stack web application with a real database, a computer vision model, faction mechanics, and a leaderboard in 35 days is not a small thing. The fact that you connected all of that to SDG education shows both technical skill and genuine care for the problem you were solving. Your pitch video was professional and your delivery was confident throughout.
The main thing holding this project back is deployment. Right now your app lives on a local machine and getting it running requires XAMPP, database configuration, and Task Scheduler setup. That means only someone with technical knowledge can actually play it. If you move the application to a cloud server, anyone with a browser and a link could join. Look into platforms like Railway or Render for the PHP side and host the Python AI server separately. That one change would make everything you built actually reachable by real users.
One more thing for next time: whenever a project uses a camera, include a short explanation of how that data is handled. Judges and users both want to know nothing is being stored or misused without consent. A few sentences in your documentation would go a long way.
The concept is one of the most creative in this challenge. The code is real, the thinking is solid, and the ambition is there. Keep building on this.
