scores.comments_panel:
Smart Bill targets a real problem. Young people don't read legislative bills, misinformation about laws spreads fast on social media, and the team's written narrative includes a specific example about anti-trans legislation passing without enough public pushback because people simply didn't know about it. That example shows genuine awareness of why the problem matters. SDG 16 is the right choice. The idea of combining LegiScan API with AI-generated plain language summaries and always linking back to the official government source is a sound approach, and the official source link is actually a good instinct for reducing misinformation risk.
That said, the submission has serious problems. Both API keys are written in plain text directly in the Python file that was submitted. This is not a minor oversight. Anyone with access to the code can use those credentials. There is no discussion of bias, accuracy, or ethical considerations anywhere in the submission. During the demo, the presenter says he is pretty sure the AI is also pulling information from the internet beyond the LegiScan data. That one sentence is a significant problem: the team does not fully understand what their own tool is doing, which matters enormously when the entire purpose of the app is to fight misinformation. The model name in the code does not exist. The search is hardcoded to aloha. No user testing is documented, no iteration, and the app is not deployed. The concept has real potential but the current state is an early prototype.
scores.comments_student:
Boston, Jazz, and Adrian, the problem you picked is worth solving and the anti-trans legislation example in your team story shows you understand the real stakes. Using AI to turn dense legal language into four readable sentences is a genuinely good idea, and always linking back to the official source is the right instinct.
Two things need to be fixed before anything else. Your API keys are visible in the code you submitted. That means anyone who reads the file can use your Gemini and LegiScan accounts. Look up environment variables and .env files: that is the standard way to keep keys out of your code. It takes about 20 minutes to learn and fix. Second, during the demo you said you were not sure whether the AI was pulling information from outside LegiScan. You need to know the answer to that. Your whole app is built on the premise of giving people accurate information about real bills. If the AI is adding things you did not give it, you have a misinformation problem inside the tool that is supposed to fight misinformation. Fix the search so users can type any term, secure the keys, deploy it somewhere, and get 5 real people to test it. That version of this project would be much harder to say no to.

ISTE26AI193.ai_tools_used:
Artificial intelligence plays a central role in the development of our solution, both as a core component of the product and as a supportive tool throughout the design and development process.

Our solution leverages AI to analyze user inputs, identify behavioral and emotional patterns, and enable personalized experiences aimed at improving social well-being. These AI-driven processes are designed and controlled within a human-centered framework, where all system logic, ethical boundaries, and decision-making criteria are defined by the team.

During the ideation and development phases, multiple AI tools were used responsibly to enhance productivity and insight generation. ChatGPT and Gemini were utilized to support problem exploration, system design discussions, content refinement, and clarity in communication. Gemini was also explored for image and visual verification tasks, supporting accuracy and consistency during visual development stages.

Perplexity AI was used as a research assistant to cross-check information, explore existing approaches, and validate assumptions through structured summaries of reliable sources. This allowed the team to make more informed decisions while maintaining independent critical evaluation.

Manus AI was employed for early-stage prototyping, workflow modeling, and rapid iteration of possible system structures. By simulating different flows and interactions, Manus helped us test ideas efficiently before implementation.

Across all stages, artificial intelligence functioned as an accelerator and analytical supportâ€”not as an autonomous decision-maker. The team retained full ownership of the concept, system architecture, ethical responsibility, and final outcomes. All AI-generated insights were reviewed, filtered, and adapted by the team to align with our values and project goals.

By combining AI-powered analysis with human judgment, empathy, and oversight, our project demonstrates a responsible and impactful use of artificial intelligence to address real-world social challenges.

round2_scores.comments_panel: SocialWellBeing earned the strongest AI ethics score (4 on Criterion 3) based on an exceptionally detailed and transparent AI use disclosure. The team names four AI tools with specific purposes, draws an explicit boundary between AI as "accelerator" versus decision-maker, and builds privacy protections directly into the product (photo verification with no face recognition, no identity tracking, no data storage; MBTI assessments framed as non-diagnostic). The concept of using AI to guide socially withdrawn individuals back into real-world interaction, rather than deeper into screens, reflects genuine creative thinking (Criterion 4). The Team Story clearly defines three complementary roles and connects the problem to personal experience (Criterion 6). No SDG named (Criterion 1). Video is voiceover only, no team members on camera (Criterion 7).Two concerns for the panel's consideration. First, the YouTube title reads "İntel Skills For İnnovation-SociallWellBeing" and the video was uploaded January 31, 2026, roughly six weeks before other submissions. This suggests the project may have been originally developed for an Intel education program. The panel should determine whether this constitutes a cross-submission concern under Challenge rules. Second, the prototype is hosted on Manus.space with a "Made with Manus" badge, meaning an AI agent assisted in building the application. The team is transparent about this in their disclosure, but it raises questions about the degree of student-built implementation versus AI-generated output that the panel may want to weigh.I am recommending this project move forward based on the strength of the concept, the AI ethics framework, and the team's thoughtful design, but I want these concerns visible to the panel.
round2_scores.comments_student:
Your AI use disclosure is the most detailed and transparent we reviewed. Naming each tool, explaining its specific purpose, and drawing a clear line between AI assistance and human decision-making is exactly the level of thoughtfulness judges look for. The privacy protections built into your product (no face recognition, no identity tracking, no data storage for photo verification) show you took ethical design seriously at the feature level, not just in a written statement.
The concept of guiding socially withdrawn individuals back into real-world interaction through small, achievable, rewarded actions is creative and psychologically sound. Your Team Story effectively shows how three distinct skill sets complement each other.
Two areas to strengthen: name your specific UN SDG(s) (SDG 3 is a clear fit for this project), and consider appearing on camera in your pitch video. The narration is emotionally compelling, but judges connect more deeply when they see the team behind the work, especially for a project rooted in personal experience with social isolation.

Critical Digital Product Failure: I strongly urge you to test the actual digital product link provided by the team. While their essay claims they conducted "extensive trial-and-error testing" and found "no discrepancies" when cross-checking locations globally, real-world testing reveals the app suffers from heavy hallucinations. The AI frequently generates inaccurate locations and entirely fabricated websites.

Ethical and Design Scoring: Because the target audience is highly vulnerable individuals facing food and resource insecurity, providing them with fake addresses or non-existent support resources is actively harmful. As a result, I scored both Ethical Use of AI and Design Quality & Accessibility as a 2 (Minimal evidence). The app lacks the necessary guardrails and factual grounding to be considered usable or safe for its intended purpose.

Strong Concept, Flawed Execution: The team's foundational idea is excellent, and their specific effort to mitigate bias by ensuring small, locally-owned nonprofits are highlighted over larger organizations is a great ethical consideration. However, the technical execution completely undermines these goals.
scores.comments_student:
What you did well: Your mission to combat food and resource insecurity is deeply commendable, and you have clearly identified a widespread issue affecting millions. Your written essay and pitch video were both highly professional, well-organized, and effectively communicated your vision. Additionally, your deliberate effort to adjust the AI to prevent larger organizations from overshadowing smaller, locally-owned nonprofits is exactly the kind of ethical foresight we look for in AI design!

Areas for Growth (The Prototype): In your essay, you stated that your testing demonstrated "strong stability and reliability" and that "no discrepancies were found" when verifying AI outputs. However, during my evaluation of your app, the AI suffered from severe hallucinations, frequently providing inaccurate local addresses and completely fabricating websites. When building an app for vulnerable populations—such as a single mother looking for essentials —accuracy is the most critical feature. Sending someone in need to a fake location can cause real-world harm and distress.

Next Steps for Development: To solve this hallucination issue, you cannot rely solely on the Llama 4 Scout model's internal knowledge base to generate locations. In future iterations, you will need to "ground" your AI by connecting it directly to a verified, continually updated database or API (like Google Places API) so the AI only retrieves and summarizes real data rather than inventing it. Additionally, keep in mind that community shelters or food banks in many rural areas have a poor official website presence. They often utilize only social media to advertise events and available resources. You need to ensure your AI model is explicitly instructed and capable of searching social media platforms, especially local Facebook pages, to accurately capture these local offerings. Furthermore, you must include a clear, prominent disclaimer on your interface warning users that AI can make mistakes and that they should call ahead to verify resources.

ISTE26AI207.ai_tools_used: In the paper we incorporated AI by deploying the YOLOv8 objectâ€‘detection model to recognize waste items in real time, achieving a confidence threshold ofâ€¯â‰¥â€¯0.6 before routing the material to the appropriate compartment. The model was trained on a TACOâ€‘derived image set, which provides diverse examples of biodegradable, nonâ€‘biodegradable and recyclable trash while deliberately excluding any personal or location data to safeguard privacy.

scores.comments_panel:
WASTECO is a technically ambitious project, as it's a physical smart bin system with automatic waste segregation, not just a software app.  The system detects waste items via camera using YOLOv8, which is a machine learning model performing live image classification that requires actual model implementation skills.  That the students knew to use the TACO dataset rather than some generic image dataset shows genuine domain-specific research.  Confidence threshold of 0.6 is a real implementation parameter. The fact that they cite it specifically means they actually trained, tested, and tuned the model — not just called an API.  The video makes it clear that this is a student-created project, with the group members clearly identifying their roles in the project.  

One critique is while the group describes a lived experience of watching garbage accumulate on streets, sidewalks, and canals in Metro Manila every single day, no SDG is named anywhere in the submission, even though SDG 11 and 12 both connect.

The biggest accomplishment is in Criterion 3.  They name the specific model (YOLOv8), name the specific dataset (TACO), explain why they chose it (diverse examples across three waste categories), set a specific confidence threshold (0.6) with a clear reason (accuracy before routing), and explicitly address privacy (excluding personal and location data from training). This is exactly what Criterion 3 is asking for, and none of the other teams I evaluated came close to this level of specificity.
scores.comments_student:

WASTECO is a technically ambitious project and your use of YOLOv8 and the TACO dataset demonstrates genuine domain-specific research and understanding.  Your video makes it clear that this is a student-created project and your decision to include the reflection on team challenges (balancing exams, technical failures, etc.) helps this project's story.  To improve, we would have liked to see evidence of collaboration with waste management experts, community members, or those affected by the problem.  

The hardware exists and was demonstrated on video. The iteration evidence is authentic (power system failures, repeated testing and adjusting). However, the power system problems are described as partially unresolved, there is no mention of user testing with actual community members, and the physical design constraints of hardware make accessibility considerations harder to evaluate.

Overall, this is a very impressive submission with a lot of potential.  Continue to develop this system and we hope to see it in real use someday.

scores.comments_panel:
Ho'oulu Keiki has the strongest research foundation in this group. The team connected Hawaii's 90% food import dependency and state legislation targeting 30% local sourcing by 2030 to the daily reality of students throwing away cafeteria food they don't want to eat. Interviewing the school lunch manager gave them real information that shaped the design directly. The two-portal concept is thoughtful: students share preferences through a conversational chatbot, administrators use that data to generate USDA-compliant weekly menus. Checking AI outputs against nutritional standards shows the team understood that AI can produce plausible but inaccurate nutrition guidance and took a step to address it.
The technical execution is where the submission falls short. The application runs on Claude's interface rather than as an independently coded product. The pitch video shows the app functioning in a demo context, which is partial evidence of design intent. However, during independent review the student portal got stuck and did not complete the interaction, and the admin portal did not load. Whether the app was stable at the time of recording or broke afterward cannot be determined. A product that cannot be independently verified as functional cannot be scored as fully meeting the design criteria. With two criteria at 2, the submission meets the Round 1 minimum but does not qualify for Round 2.

The way you showed the problem in the video, through real scenarios and different perspectives, made it easy to follow and care about. It is clear this is a team that actually worked together.
The app wasn't working when we tried it. The student chat got stuck and the admin portal didn't respond, so we couldn't see the full loop running. I think this can be used to enmity kids for sure, but I think there are better tools out there as a conduit for entering the problems. Chat is limited in how you enter problems so there is a certain math knowledge needed to input the data which is holding it back for me.
round2_scores.comments_student: The research supporting this project is great and something that seems like it is really needed. A consideration would be to find a platform you could hand write a problem and AI could identify the problem, as math problems are not always easy to type into ChatGPT.

round2_scores.comments_panel: The community problem section is genuinely outstanding. The student used Hawaii DOE Strive HI data, NAEP national reports, and Title 1 school breakdowns to build a data-driven case that most adult presenters would struggle to match. That research effort deserves recognition. The anti-cheating mechanism is also a thoughtful ethical design choice and demonstrates real understanding of how AI can be misused in educational settings. However, there is a significant credibility issue: the written narrative explicitly claims the system includes user interface screens and a database to store learning history, but the submitted product is a standard Custom GPT with no custom interface and no persistent database. The technical track requires actual implementation. Scores for innovation, design quality, and collaboration were moderated accordingly. The instructional thinking behind this project is strong but the technical execution does not match what was described.
round2_scores.comments_student:
Asher, the research behind this project is some of the strongest we reviewed. Using the Strive HI State Report and NAEP data to document the 29 percent achievement gap shows a level of seriousness that goes well beyond what most students attempt. You clearly understand the problem deeply and care about it.
The decision to make the AI refuse to solve the original problem and offer a parallel practice question instead is smart. It shows you thought about how students might misuse the tool and designed against it. That kind of ethical thinking matters in real technology development.
One thing to work on for future submissions is making sure your written description matches your actual product. Your narrative described a database and a custom interface, but what you submitted was a Custom GPT. There is nothing wrong with building on top of existing AI tools, but be precise about what you built and how. Judges look at the live product and read the narrative side by side.
If you can build Aloha Math AI into a real standalone platform with teacher dashboards and actual progress tracking, this idea has legs. The concept is solid. Now build the thing you described.
